bibliographic.citation: Carl Horstmann, ed., 'Questiones by-twene the Maister of Oxenford and his Clerke,' Englische Studien 8 (Heilbronn: Gebr. Henninger, 1885), 284-287.
bibliographic.publisher: Gebr. Henninger; distributed by Truebner & Co. and B. Westermann & Co.
bibliographic.title: Questiones by-twene the Maister of Oxenford and his Clerke

dateStatements.label: publication
dateStatements.notes: The current British Library record misprints this as 1855; the volume title page is dated 1885.

entities.description: The immediate source edition for CME00011: a single-witness transcription of the fuller Harley MS 1304 version, printed as a short article in Englische Studien 8.
entities.label: Horstmann, Questiones by-twene the Maister of Oxenford and his Clerke (1885)
notes: The article begins partway down p. 284 after a preceding contribution and ends partway down p. 287 before the next article.
Horstmann identifies Harley 1304 in the subtitle and cites Wuelcker's partly abbreviated Lansdowne 762 version in a footnote.
The article supplies no general editorial-method statement; it visibly uses italic type for many letter sequences, parentheses, ellipsis, and a footnote declaring one number unreadable.

dateStatements.label: current British Library catalog date
dateStatements.value: Second half of the fifteenth century

entities.description: A late-fifteenth-century English paper miscellany, with parchment additions and later binding, containing John Lydgate's Life of Our Lady on ff. 1r-99r, the fuller copy of the Master of Oxford's Catechism on ff. 100r-102v, and the ABC of Aristotle and other proverbs on ff. 103r-103v.

holding.notes: The exact work is cataloged within the parent record at ff. 100r-102v; no separate component record is exposed.

entities.label: British Library, Harley MS 1304
notes: The current record says the legacy digital images are unavailable. No complete public Presentation manifest, image inventory, PDF, or selected work-leaf images were found on 2026-07-11.
The failed public-image check does not establish that the British Library lacks internal photography, microfilm, or an orderable surrogate.

physicalDescription.dimensions: 200 x 285 mm; text space 100 x 175 mm
physicalDescription.extent: 104 folios, plus one unfoliated paper flyleaf at the beginning, two at the end, parchment f. 1*, and one unfoliated pastedown
physicalDescription.format: Codex; Gothic cursive (Secretary) script; post-1600 blind-stamped and tooled brown-leather binding
notes: The work occupies ff. 100r-102v.

A complete reproduction must retain covers or boards, spine if offered, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, cancelled-leaf evidence, every recto and verso, and adjacent works rather than only ff. 100r-102v.
The current catalog records England as origin.
physicalDescription.support: Paper; parchment f. 1*; additional paper flyleaves and pastedown

rights.jurisdiction: Image-holder copyright, provider terms, and intended publication territory
notes: The medieval text's age and physical consultation route do not determine rights in new photographs.

The complete object is required to inspect the exact parchment-to-paper boundary crossed by the work and to test the conflicting dates.

dateStatements.label: current whole-volume catalog date
dateStatements.notes: The catalog's Start Date is 1509 and End Date is 1570, but its displayed Date Range is 1509-1564; this record preserves the catalog's narrower displayed range and narrative estimate.

dateStatements.label: older exact-copy assignment
dateStatements.notes: Wright and Halliwell explicitly assign the manuscript copy to Henry V's reign, and Wuelcker repeats that placement. It conflicts with current whole-volume cataloging and remains unresolved for ff. 3v-5r.
dateStatements.value: Written in the reign of Henry V (1413-1422)

entities.description: A London Tudor commonplace book containing the shorter copy of the Master of Oxford's Catechism on ff. 3v-5r among civic, devotional, prophetic, historical, practical, and literary material.

holding.notes: The exact work is cataloged within the parent record at ff. 3v-5r; no separate component record is exposed.

entities.label: British Library, Lansdowne MS 762
notes: The current record says the legacy digital images are unavailable. No complete public Presentation manifest, image inventory, PDF, or selected work-leaf images were found on 2026-07-11.
Horstmann calls this version partly abbreviated, but the exact stemmatic direction between the two surviving copies is not inferred here.
The work occupies ff. 3v-5r and therefore crosses the cataloged support boundary between parchment through f. 4v and paper at f. 5r.
A complete reproduction must retain covers or boards, spine if offered, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, every recto and verso, and adjacent commonplace-book material rather than only ff. 3v-5r.
The current catalog gives London as origin for the enclosing manuscript.
physicalDescription.support: Paper, with parchment at ff. 1r-4v, 29r-33v, 61r-67v, 86r-94v, and 98r-99v

rights.basis: No image deliverable or item-level image license was available. British Library Digitisation Services separates image supply from reproduction and licensing enquiries.
rights.component: Any British Library manuscript photographs or supplied complete-codex reproduction
rights.jurisdiction: Image-holder copyright, provider terms, and intended publication territory
notes: The medieval text's age and physical consultation route do not determine rights in new photographs.

bibliographic.citation: Thomas Wright and James Orchard Halliwell, eds., 'The Master of Oxford's Catechism,' in Reliquiae Antiquae, vol. 1 (London: John Russell Smith, 1845), 230-232.
bibliographic.title: The Master of Oxford's Catechism

dateStatements.label: publication
dateStatements.notes: The inspected volume title page is dated 1845; any earlier part-issue date remains unresolved.

entities.description: The earliest verified collected-volume publication of the work, printing the shorter Lansdowne MS 762 text in Reliquiae Antiquae volume 1.
entities.label: Wright and Halliwell, The Master of Oxford's Catechism (1845)
notes: The edition explicitly says it is from Lansdowne MS 762 and supplies the now-conventional catechism title.
Its statement that the manuscript was written in Henry V's reign is retained as an older assertion, not silently reconciled with the current British Library date.

bibliographic.citation: Richard Paul Wuelcker, ed., 'Zwiegespraech zwischen einem Oxorder Lehrer und dessen Schueler,' in Altenglisches Lesebuch, vol. 2 (Halle: Max Niemeyer, 1879), 191-194.
bibliographic.title: Zwiegespraech zwischen einem Oxorder Lehrer und dessen Schueler

entities.description: A later lineated edition of the shorter Lansdowne MS 762 version in volume 2 of Wuelcker's Altenglisches Lesebuch.
entities.label: Wuelcker, Zwiegespraech zwischen einem Oxorder Lehrer und dessen Schueler (1879)

bibliographic.citation: Hans Sauer and Alessia Bauer, eds., To Instruct and to Entertain: Medieval Didactic Dialogues, Middle English Texts 67 (Heidelberg: Universitaetsverlag Winter, 2023), lv + 144 pp.
bibliographic.title: To Instruct and to Entertain: Medieval Didactic Dialogues

entities.description: A modern parallel critical edition that prints the Master of Oxford's Catechism from Harley 1304 and Lansdowne 762 alongside the Old English Prose Solomon and Saturn and a reconstructed Latin source.
entities.label: Sauer and Bauer, To Instruct and to Entertain (2023)
notes: This later comparison edition is not upstream of Horstmann or CME00011.
The reconstructed Latin is an editorial hypothesis; no surviving Latin witness to that source is represented as a lineage entity here.

bibliographic.citation: Englische Studien, vol. 8 (1885; first reprinting, New York and London: Johnson Reprint Corporation and Johnson Reprint Company Limited, 1965).
bibliographic.publisher: Johnson Reprint Corporation; Johnson Reprint Company Limited
bibliographic.title: Englische Studien

entities.description: The first Johnson reprinting of the complete 1885 volume, retaining the original title page, contents, pagination, and Horstmann article while adding 1965 reprint statements.
entities.label: Johnson Reprint Corporation reprint of Englische Studien 8 (1965)
notes: The scan inspected for this manifest is of this reprint, not proof that U-M keyed from this exact physical copy.

next_steps: Ask British Library Digitisation Services whether the legacy complete object can be restored or supplied and whether a new complete-codex order is feasible.
Specify covers or boards, spine if offered, pastedowns, all flyleaves and blanks, cancelled-leaf evidence, and every recto and verso through f. 104; identify ff. 100r-102v only as the work mapping.
If a complete object becomes public, cache the entire deliverable or IIIF canvas sequence and record exact start/end canvases without cropping to the work.
Keep research access, reproduction supply, and publication permission separate.
notes: The current catalog says the legacy digital images are unavailable; both tested public IIIF routes also failed. No selected-leaf substitute was found.
openQuestions.question: Can a complete public or repository-supplied photographic object of Harley MS 1304 be obtained and mapped to the work at ff. 100r-102v?

next_steps: Ask British Library Digitisation Services for the legacy complete object or a new complete-codex reproduction, including covers, endleaves, blanks, all additional leaves, and every recto and verso.
Map the work to ff. 3v-5r and inspect the parchment-to-paper boundary at 4v/5r, changes of hand, quire structure, inks, and any evidence for an inserted older unit.
Compare the physical evidence with Wright and Halliwell's Henry V claim and the current whole-volume 1509-1564 catalog date.
If only selected leaves are eventually released, cache permitted files as coverage=partial and retain the negative whole-object finding.
openQuestions.question: Can a complete image sequence and component-level codicological analysis of Lansdowne MS 762 resolve the exact date, support transition, and textual extent of the copy on ff. 3v-5r?

next_steps: Seek item-level U-M production metadata or a raw distribution package that identifies the physical or digital keying copy.
Treat the collection-level manual-keying statement and 2016 TEMPHEAD review as context until exact production evidence is available.
Continue to cite Horstmann's 1885 edition as the textual source while keeping the inspected 1965 reproduction layer separate.
openQuestions.question: Did U-M key CME00011 from an original 1885 copy, the 1965 Johnson reprint, or another reproduction, and what item-level correction history followed?

reviewNotes: The complete cached 1845, 1879, and 1965 printed-volume PDFs were opened and checked for identity, title pages, article boundaries, work-page mapping, and distinguishing reprint matter from original publication matter.
The cached scan-data XML files were used only as digital page maps. Their metadata_only coverage does not imply image coverage.
Whole-manuscript negative findings are dated and scoped to publicly obtainable files and IIIF routes. They do not assert that the British Library lacks internal, onsite, or orderable reproductions.
No manuscript image excerpt was substituted for a complete physical object, and no provider-restricted image bytes were downloaded.

lineage.summary: The repository XML copies University of Michigan item CME00011, an encoding of Carl Horstmann's 1885 four-page edition of the fuller Harley MS 1304 text. Horstmann directly identifies Harley 1304, ff. 100r-102v, and points to Wuelcker's edition of the shorter Lansdowne MS 762 copy, now cataloged at ff. 3v-5r. Wright and Halliwell first printed that shorter copy in 1845. Modern scholarship edits Harley and Lansdowne in parallel. The available Internet Archive source for Horstmann is a complete scan of a 1965 reprint of Englische Studien 8, not a manuscript facsimile. Both British Library legacy digital-object links are currently unavailable, so no public complete-codex or selected-leaf image object was cached.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: Traditionally assigned to the early fifteenth century; exact composition and earliest-copy dates unresolved
catalogSummary.region: Place of composition unknown; the Harley copy is from England and the Lansdowne miscellany is associated with London
catalogSummary.title: The Master of Oxford's Catechism

catalogingSubject.label: The Master of Oxford's Catechism
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English prose question-and-answer work conventionally called The Master of Oxford's Catechism, represented here by the fuller Harley MS 1304 version. The subject includes the sequence of exchanges between a clerk and master but excludes Harley MS 1304 and Lansdowne MS 762 as physical miscellanies, their distinct textual copies, the 1845, 1879, 1885, 1965, and 2023 editions or reprints, the University of Michigan encoding, and the repository XML artifact.

assertion.notes: Wright and Halliwell and Wuelcker assign the Lansdowne copy to Henry V's reign, but the current British Library catalog dates the enclosing commonplace book to 1509-1564 and probably the 1520s or 1530s. The traditional date is therefore not normalized into exact years.

dateStatements.display: Traditionally early fifteenth century; exact date unresolved
notes: A manuscript-copy date, even if resolved, would not by itself establish the composition date.

assertion.notes: Harley is securely dated 1450-1499. The older claim that Lansdowne 762 was written under Henry V could make that copy earlier, but it conflicts with current whole-volume cataloging and has not been resolved at the exact ff. 3v-5r component level.

dateStatements.display: Harley MS 1304 is securely dated to the second half of the fifteenth century; the exact date of the Lansdowne copy remains disputed

assertion.notes: This is the earliest printed edition verified in this review. The publication history of Reliquiae Antiquae in parts was not resolved, so an earlier part-issue date remains possible.

dateStatements.display: 1845 (earliest verified collected-volume edition, from Lansdowne MS 762)

scope.description: Wright and Halliwell's shorter Lansdowne version

extent.description: The Harley-version XML contains one DIV1 catechism with 100 alternating SP elements, representing 50 clerk/master question-and-answer exchanges. It contains 121 P, 4 PB, 2 editorial NOTE, and 1 GAP elements. The extra paragraph elements divide enumerated answers and are encoding metrics, not additional exchanges. The shorter Lansdowne version is not included in these counts.

formStatements.description: A prose dialogue of alternating questions by a clerk and answers by a master; enumerated answers sometimes span several paragraph elements.

genres.label: Question-and-answer literature
notes: The sequence combines instruction with paradoxical questions, wisdom sayings, and riddling lore.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The XML correctly marks TEXT/@LANG='enm'. German editorial headings and footnotes belong to Horstmann's edition rather than the medieval work.
The British Library catalog's 1855 date for Horstmann is a bibliographic error; Englische Studien volume 8 is dated 1885 on its title page.
The word Oxford in the conventional title identifies a dialogue role and is not used as evidence for place of composition.
The fifty-exchange count describes the fuller Harley version as represented by CME00011, not the shorter Lansdowne copy.

next_steps: Consult Sauer and Bauer's 2023 dating, dialect, and stemmatic discussion in full and any subsequent specialist corrections.
Do not infer Oxford composition from the master's title or transfer Harley's English origin and Lansdowne's London association to the abstract work.
Resolve the older Henry V dating of Lansdowne ff. 3v-5r against current codicological cataloging before treating it as the earliest witness.
openQuestions.question: When and where was the work composed, and which linguistic features belong to the work rather than the Harley or Lansdowne scribes?

next_steps: Collate the two current manuscript copies or Sauer and Bauer's complete parallel apparatus, not only their nineteenth-century editions.
Keep the reconstructed Latin source hypothetical unless a physical witness is identified.
Do not model the shorter Lansdowne version as a direct abbreviation of Harley without stemmatic evidence.
openQuestions.question: What exact textual relation connects the fuller Harley copy, the shorter Lansdowne copy, the Prose Solomon and Saturn, and the reconstructed common Latin source?

assertion.notes: The master and clerk are dialogue roles, not identified historical authors; no author name is supplied by the witnesses or accepted scholarship reviewed.

scope.description: The 1885 Harley MS 1304 edition used by the U-M encoding

subjects.label: Creation, cosmology, and the natural world
Christian doctrine and the Ten Commandments

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English prose dialogue in which a clerk questions a master through fifty exchanges. The fuller Harley version moves from creation, Adam, biblical genealogy, Noah and the Flood to cities, plants, animals, waters, fallen angels, the elements, the soul, Moses and the Ten Commandments, proverbial judgments, natural lore, and cosmological riddles. Its brief question-and-answer units combine Christian instruction with apocryphal, encyclopedic, and entertaining wisdom material.

The work survives in two British Library copies. Harley MS 1304, ff. 100r-102v, preserves the fuller version edited by Horstmann and encoded as CME00011. Lansdowne MS 762, ff. 3v-5r, preserves a shorter version first printed by Wright and Halliwell and later by Wuelcker. Modern scholarship edits the two copies in parallel and relates the work closely to the Old English Prose Solomon and Saturn, while treating their common Latin source as a reconstruction rather than a surviving witness.

assertion.notes: This is the established scholarly title, not a demonstrated authorial title.

scope.description: Horstmann's 1885 article title and the form used for the Harley copy in the current British Library catalog
